neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert and oriented x4, slightly confused post-ictally
neurological_exam.sensory: Intact to light touch and pinprick bilaterally
neurological_exam.reflexes: 2+ and symmetric throughout, plantar responses flexor bilaterally
neurological_exam.additional: Tongue bite noted on left lateral tongue

patient.history_present_illness: 32-year-old right-handed male presented to the ED after a witnessed generalized tonic-clonic seizure lasting approximately 2 minutes. His wife reports that prior to the convulsion, the patient appeared to stare blankly and made chewing motions with his mouth for about 30 seconds, followed by left arm stiffening and then bilateral convulsive activity. She also reports that over the past 3 months, the patient has described intermittent episodes of an intense feeling of déjà vu lasting 30-60 seconds, sometimes accompanied by a rising sensation in his stomach. These episodes occur 2-3 times per week and have been increasing in frequency. No prior seizure history. No recent head trauma, fever, or illness.

background.pdr: 9 Hz alpha rhythm, symmetric and reactive
background.sleep_features: Normal sleep architecture with vertex waves and sleep spindles
background.overall: Well-organized background with superimposed focal abnormalities

findings.frequency: Intermittent, approximately 2-3 per minute during drowsiness
findings.morphology: Phase-reversing sharp waves with after-going slow wave, 100-150 ms duration
findings.state: Predominantly during drowsiness and light sleep
findings.clinical_correlation: Consistent with right anterior temporal epileptogenic focus

findings.state: Present during wakefulness, more prominent during drowsiness
findings.clinical_correlation: Suggests underlying structural or functional abnormality in right temporal region

activating_procedures.photic_stimulation: Normal photic driving response, no photoparoxysmal response

eeg.impression: Abnormal EEG showing right anterior temporal sharp waves and intermittent right temporal slowing, consistent with a right temporal epileptogenic focus.
eeg.limitations: Routine 30-minute study; prolonged monitoring may reveal additional findings.
recommended_actions: Consider prolonged video-EEG monitoring if seizures recur

findings.location: Right mesial temporal lobe, centered in the amygdala/anterior hippocampus

signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense with surrounding subtle FLAIR signal

findings.mass_effect: Mild, with slight effacement of the temporal horn of the right lateral ventricle

mri.impression: Well-circumscribed right mesial temporal mass centered in the amygdala/anterior hippocampus region. Imaging characteristics suggest a low-grade neuroepithelial tumor, most likely a dysembryoplastic neuroepithelial tumor (DNET) or ganglioglioma.

differential_by_imaging.features: Cortically based, T2 hyperintense, no enhancement, well-circumscribed
Similar imaging appearance, may show calcification

recommended_actions: Consider MR spectroscopy for additional characterization
Follow-up MRI in 3 months if surgery not immediately planned

BMP.clinical_significance: Mildly elevated, likely post-ictal stress response

Special.clinical_significance: Elevated, consistent with post-ictal state (supports true epileptic seizure vs. psychogenic event)

labs.interpretation: Labs largely unremarkable except for mildly elevated glucose (likely post-ictal stress) and significantly elevated prolactin consistent with a true epileptic seizure.

temporal lobe epilepsy DNET surgical out.query: temporal lobe epilepsy DNET surgical outcomes

results.finding: Seizure freedom rate of 80% after lesionectomy for DNET-associated temporal lobe epilepsy
Complete resection of DNET achieves Engel Class I outcome in >85% of patients
Early surgical referral recommended for drug-resistant focal epilepsy with a structural lesion

temporal lobe epilepsy DNET surgical out.summary: Strong evidence supports surgical treatment for DNET-associated temporal lobe epilepsy, with seizure freedom rates of 80-85% after complete resection. Early surgical referral is recommended.

warnings: Monitor for behavioral side effects (irritability, aggression)
Dose adjustment needed in renal impairment

findings.location: Right anterior temporal onset (F8/T4), rapid spread to right hemisphere
findings.morphology: Low-voltage fast activity evolving to rhythmic theta, then generalization
findings.clinical_correlation: Confirms right anterior temporal seizure onset zone, concordant with MRI lesion location

output.impression: Video-EEG confirms right anterior temporal seizure onset zone with 2 captured clinical seizures. Excellent concordance with MRI lesion.

ground_truth.primary_diagnosis: Focal epilepsy secondary to right mesial temporal mass (likely DNET)

differential.key_features: Similar imaging, would require pathology to differentiate
Elevated prolactin and EEG findings argue against

optimal_actions.action: Search literature on DNET and surgical outcomes

key_reasoning_points: Recognize focal onset based on semiology (staring, oral automatisms, left arm stiffening before GTC)
Identify right temporal localization from EEG (anterior temporal sharp waves)
Correlate EEG focus with MRI lesion (right mesial temporal mass)
Recognize DNET as most likely lesion based on imaging characteristics
Recommend neurosurgical evaluation given drug-resistant potential
Start appropriate AED (levetiracetam) with interaction check
